name: Macbeth
comment:
Where do I begin? 
This is a poetry of pixels no less. 
This story, this epic of how a few down to earth kids banded together to create the mesh of wit, creative valour and imagniation, has sent me bouncing off the walls. for some reason i had an instant and deep felt connection with these people, these guys and girls.
My eyes lit up with awe at the early concept works, and are continuing to swim around in my concious as I type this.
these creative and crafty masterminds, (that had me almost at wits ends in parts of portal2), I'd tip my hat to you...if i had one but i dont, so ill use this crab looking thing i found on some guys head...
reading the origins of valve, of portal, is so nostalgic, its that felling of "wow this is where it all began" like you loking throught a, well, time PORTAL if you will, into the fragile beginings of the magestic creation that is portal 2.
now i wont lie, i have become increasingly emulsified, and obssessed by this games world, I can close my eyes and run through chambers, excursion tunnels and make physics ma bitch....and you know what,i dont care if that makes me "geeky" its bloody amazing is what it is! and im soo glad that I could finally play a game that had me a the point of "gnah, ok, I MUST finish this puzzle, I CAN finish this puzzle...otherwise GLaDOS will call me names again :(" 
and its not very often that a) i can find a game really able to stimulate my level of cognitive ability
and 2) made me think "omg, these actually guys think like I do, we definately need to hang"
Valve has a relationship with its gamers, something thats created and grown not bought and sold, and personally Im so bloody glad to be a part of it.
my explsoive passion, enthrallment and love for this game actually cannot be expressed in words..
I cant wait for whats in store ^^

name: Voodooman
comment:
Ending of Portal 2 was completely ruined and stupid, i mean this whole pre-rendered turret song sequence and sunny field.
1) prerendered cut-scenes NO GO! 30 fps, compression artifacts and 1280x720 resolution + different gamma looks bad and breaking immersion because of lack of interactivity - i wanted to move the camera at least. Only real-time cut-scenes is way to go.
2) Ending itself just a nonsence (as well as the beggining) - stupid singing turrets, chell in a middle of field many years after combine invasion, wihtout food and gun - im 99% she will be dead in a next few days. Anyway this whole video looked stupid and out of place, too much humor, nothing serious, nothing as good as portal 1 ending was. I really wish Valve to retcon this ending in DLC and remake it completely almost like this did with ending of Portal 1. 
Ending of Co-op have pretty same problems - people locked near test chambers of 1950 era makes no sence, especially when technology inside Vault dont look like 1950 era - what the heck Erick was thinking of? It seem from this book that story development was pretty troubled because Vavle do levels 1st, add story second, and here we have results. Portal 2 was good gameplay-wice, but it have a lot of plot mistakes, nonsence and too much humor that was out-of-place. Portal 1 was better than this and was more serious.

name: Noel
comment:
Very good book. Maybe a bit self-flattering but still very alluminating to the process of making the game. I'm sure each other game has an interesting backstory to it too. 

I think you should maybe make a movie/comic/animation/book about the prequal story you guys had origonally planned for protal 2.  If it didn't work as a game then maybe these mediums would be better. Might be a cool story to see/read.

Also too bad you knocked the "Museam" bit out of Portal 2 as it looked interesting.

Really enjoyed the game both in singleplayer and Co-op.  My brother is a huge portal fan so it was a really special treat to be able to play co-op with him.  It's one of the few multiplayer games we can play together as he's not a shooting fan like I am. It some ways it was kinda nice to play co-op cause sometimes my brian would get tired so I'd let him solve a puzzle or vice versa.  That last puzzle in section 4 nearly broke our sanity as we sat on it for about an hour and a half until I had an epiphany with it. The solution struck me as being typical Valve humor. Silly Valve!!

My bro and I still refect on that puzzle and how we had that breaking moment together.

name: Phillipe Bosher
comment:
Having only watched Gametrailers TV a small number of times, and hearing of Geoff Keighley's apparent tantrums with regards to some smaller members of the games press, I was a little unsure about buying this app. After all, how could someone who, to my limited knowledge, was a TV presenter with an attitude problem possibly create a piece of literature that I was interested in?

I have to say, I was blown away by the quality of the storytelling, the subtle nuances in the way in which the story was told, and the overall quality of the app. Bravo, Mr Keighley, you have achieved something I have only ever hoped for - an interesting look at a video game that focuses as much on the people and the team making it as the game itself. Thank you!

Thank you so much, Geoff Keighley and everyone else who contributed to this insight. I deeply appreciate your work and commitment to tell the story behind Portal 2.

name: Takkie
comment:
Portal 2 has to be one of the greatest games I've ever played.  I've always been a die-hard console/hand-held fan, but my friend introduced me into the wonderful world of Steam via a free download of Portal.  Yes, Portal really started it all for me.  

That friend, my now best friend and boyfriend, still plays games with me to this day.  He got me Portal 2 for Valentine's Day, and I anxiously awaited to play it alongside him over a Skype call.  Being long distance relationship with him, gaming as brought us even closer together.  Playing the single-player side of Portal 2 along side of him (he already beat it, the little over-achiever) was immesely fun.  Once I finished the story, we dove into Portal 2's co-op, which we were both very pleased with.  It was really challenging, and a good amount of the puzzles took both of our minds to figure out.  

I've always loved reading "making of" books for my favorite movies, so when I realized that one was made for Portal 2 (and was two dollars!) I axiously awaited to come home from vacation and purchase it.  I've sat here for two+ hours now reading it cover-to-cover, watching all the videos, and enjoying all the pictures.  Thanks for writing something so interactive, informative, and beautiful.

An immensely intriguing insight to the job of a games development team. For me, that would have been an interesting role-reversal: seeing what puzzles puzzled the puzzlers and how they eventually solved them.
I would like to clarify that I thouroughly enjoyed this magazine, however, and would gladly purchase a similar product that covered other Valve games.

name: Andrew
comment:
If anything, reading this has taught me how important dynamics are in development. It's really a shame that most games are either making a strong grip on proven means and grounds, being nothing more than horizontal additions to an already derrivative industry, while others venture into the creative depths without ever stopping to put order into chaos and re-evaluate. 

Portal 2 is none of those - that's exactly why it came out brilliant.

However, I'd rather see F-Stop tailored before Portal 3 or HL3 ever come out. If there's anything that the first Portal confirmed, it's that games create necessity, not the other way around. So venturing forth to a completely original IP, even one connected to Aperture Laboratories, involving no portals whatsoever, seems the next logical step.

Congrats on the hard work and its fantastic results, I'll be keeping my eyes on you.

I would like to say "thank you," to all the people at Valve for their commitment to constant innovation of the medium that's brought me so much joy, and helped me through a good deal of hardship.

And I'd also like to extend thanks to Geoff Keighley, for bringing this story to light.

Gabe, if you ever feel like you need an Accounting student for some crazy-ass reason, let me know. Steam ID is ZhaWarudo. I'm not the best, but I'm damn near to it.

name: Eduardo López Hinton
comment:
Well, how to start?
Maybe with my Steam recommendation: "I'm not only recommending a game, but a Digital Book: videos, perfect structuration of texts, polls, dynamic images, and all that is behind the creation of a masterpiece. Just for seeing how Valve is from inside, to see the human side, human persons working hard to do their best (what is -or should be- part of every job)... it's absolutely worth it. We, gamers, we only see the surface of a lot of efford and work, and we jugde and sentece with no mercy... And here is where you can realise how much has been putted into it, and how proud they can be of the job done".
And I would like to add that I'm very impressed by Erik Wolpaw. I don't know much about programming, I do play videogames since the very beginning (I'm 37), sometimes they have been an escape, a substitute to reality, and sometimes -like this times-, I start to see the games like some new forms of Literature. I do study Literature (Spanish), and I comitted my life to Storytelling, and the pure joy of listen, tell, read, watch, (and yes, play) amazing, deep and touching stories. I do have ulcerative colitis since I was 18, and I felt very impressed by Erik Wolpaw. I do understand his hesitations, his lack of forces, and the effords he had to do and how much he had to suffer to achieve his objectives. 
I would like him to know that I profoundly admire him, and how he tried to do his best, no matter his circumstances. 

And I am very glad all of you did what you have done. 
You can be very proud of it, and I am very glad of knowing you. This Digital Book has showed me a lot from you. I have enjoyed every single moment of it, and I thank all of you for that.

Well, nothing else.
Admiring you sincerely,
Eduardo López Hinton.

name: DaFreak
comment:
God ><
Talk about a cliffhanger. Can't wait to look back at this stoneage in 5 years while playing this top secret project Gabe mentioned... although, since this is Valve we are talking about, we should probably make that 7 or 8 years right? ;)

Bring it on Valve! Love you guys & girls for putting yourself through the crunch for us. Respect! Please don't ever get sick of making these awesome games for i know i will never get sick of playing them. 

On a sidenote though;You scared me quite a bit when mentioning that you are going to stop making games with an isolated SP component. Versus and co op modes are great but other people always distract from the story even if it's your best friend. You guys are the masters, the kings, no the godkings(!) of story telling. I like L4D and TF2 and portal 2s Co-op mode. They r better than most other games out there in their own right. But they can't hold a candle to the works of art you craft in SP. I hope you realize how special you guys are in that playing field and aren't planning on throwing that out of the window in favor of fun. I want to play more movies :( I don't just want to have fun. I want to cry damnit ><

Episode 2 and especially ep2s ending was the most epic, mind blowing experience i have ever had the pleasure of experiencing in a videogame. Tell more stories. Toy with our emotions. Make our jaws hit the floor. (no pressure :p)

PS: I can't not mention it sorry... where is ep3/HL3? :p

comment:
One of the things that really struck me about this story was how the Jewish employees, people who you would think would be "pure," came up with a game that alluded to "Two girls, One cup." Doesn't that go against pretty much everything they believe in? Anyway, overall I have to say that it was a great read. 

Oh and one comment towards my feelings towards Valve single player games. The one thing that has consistently been a let down for me is that the single player games always left me wanting more. Most multiplayer games, like Left 4 Dead 2, have the replayability factor that single player games just don't have. I really like the Co-op in Portal 2 and how it has the potential to expand, so I was wondering if that would be possible with Half Life 3. I don't mean like a new story line, but the ability to play the game in co-op mode and the option for the community to make more co-op levels for it would be really cool. There's a game called synergy that tries to do half-life 2 co-op but it's super glitchy and there are barely any servers that support it. So,in conclusion,  it would be pretty awesome if valve could make single player  games have a multiplayer option.

Other than those details, though, I loved the book and I felt like I learned a lot. I really hope you've backed up those youtube videos though because I'm afraid they might not exist anymore if I were to reread this in a few years.

Glad you wrote this, glad Valve was so generous in the information they gave and the stories they allowed you to tell, and glad it was provided at a price that a poor college student like myself can afford after spending half my month's budget on making so that my friends and I can play TF2 together this summer.

Thanks so much for the article, hope my criticism doesn't come off too harshly. Like I might (or might not) have said before, I'm simply glad to have this to be able to read and learn about the creators of the games I love so much.

Thank you!

name: C. L. Brown
comment:
I have only one "bone to pick" with what I've seen here...

I know that people keep claiming that "online, cooperative" is the "wave of the future" and this book mentioned that Portal 2 may be the last game with a "completely single-player mode."

This is a common claim from those who don't have "real lives" outside of the gaming world.  I've been hearing this ever since Quake's online mode, honestly.

I HOPE that is not true.  Because I have absolutely ZERO interest in "online, cooperative" play.  This is not to denigrate those who play online games, and love them.  There is certainly a market for that.  But it is not the SAME market.  And as much as technology may evolve, the MARKET is not changing... only the tools used to satisfy the needs of the market.

I want to play whenever I want to, and to be able to walk away any time I want to.  I want to use games as an escape...  to be the "star of the movie" at times and circumstances of my own choosing.  THAT is why I play  games, and why I love Half-Life and Portal and so forth.

Make me dependent on real-world scheduling...  put me into a world where I have to juggle work, dinner, and gaming...  and GAMING WILL BE LEFT BEHIND.

So...  to the folks at Valve... PLEASE do not do this.  If you do, you will simply lose me (and people like me, which I honestly believe make up the majority of players, albeit not the most vocal segment among players) as a future customer.

Portal 2's single-player mode is PERFECT.  I loved it.  I have not yet played and I have no plans at this time ever to play the "cooperative" mode, however.  It's not that I wouldn't enjoy it, if I had a "virtual cooperative player" I could switch on or off at my leisure...  but you can't do that with real people, can you?

So please...  don't "change the model" as the last few pages here inferred is the plan.  Puh-lease?

name: Yan V
comment:
To Geoff Keighley and co-creators,

Phenomenal job on weaving the production process of Portal 2 into that of a riveting tale filled with some of the most memorable individuals in the industry. I began to make progress in this app after my third run through of the game, placing aside a fourth to learn about the game I had recently fallen in love with. Needless to say, I was enthralled by the storytelling and delighted when navigating the different mediums of media alongside the text. I have a newfound appreciation not only for Portal 2 but also for Gabe, Valve, and all the individuals working to bring creativity and fun to the industry and the gamers. Furthermore, this iPad app sets a precedent in journalism on iOS and tablets, in general. Any future releases by your team will be purchased without a second thought. 

Thank you for the wonderful experience,
Yan

comment:
Great story!

Very exciting, and the wealth of pictures and multimedia was a joy. I particularly liked the character introductions at the start, for the Nuclear Monkey team; I'd have liked to see something similar for the Tag team too. 

My only gripe was that I was surprised I'd reached the end. I understand the point of the story was the experience of getting there, the uncertainty of the reception, but I'd have liked a coda that described the games release, possibly including the PSN issues -- but focusing more on the sense of resolution and closure the principle cast might receive from the final launch. As it is, we leave them when they're exhausted and uncertain, in the waiting room while their baby is in surgery, so yo speak.

Still, though, a gripping piece and a great insight into a fantastic creative process.

Why were things like this not covered?  People want to know how that idea came about.  Regardless, it's still a great read, so good infact it left me wanting more, thus my complaint.

Guess maybe I should spend a few more hours per day in the sunlight above GLaDOS' chamber and away from my beloved test chambers. I hope that maybe one day, either when Half-Life 2: Episode 3 or Half-Life 3 or that other secret project finally come out, we can maybe get another one of these interactive "The Final Hours of..." stories. These stories, the stories of the developers, the struggles, the changes that occur while developing a game, are really what makes video game development astounding, fascinating, and an incredible, collaborative art form. Let's put that in perspective shall we? 
A chocolate bar will cost you about the same as this app. 
This app will provide you with more entertainment. 
This app with provide you with hours of reading and exploring. 
This app will provide you with more informative information about Valve.

Can a chocolate bar do that? I thought not!

Plus a chocolate bar will have calories. This app has 0 calories!
